summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PROPERTY – Where first respondent wife and her adult son, the second respondent, claimed real property held on trust by wife for adult son – Where trust case abandoned on the last day of trial – Where wife says it is not just and equitable to make a s 79 Order – Where husband seeks a 50-50 adjustment of property – Where just and equitable to make an order – Where s 79 adjustment Order made – Where property to be sold – Where valuation old and wife has no capacity to pay out the husband

subseq.zh: 标准层(自动摘要)。完整精读可后续升级。
subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

BRASCH J:

The applicant husband is 73 years old, having been born in 1952. The first respondent wife is soon 79 years old, having been born in 1947.
The husband and wife commenced cohabitation in mid-1996 and were married in November 1997. They finally separated in either late 2017 or early 2018 and were divorced in 2019.
The second respondent is the wife’s adult son from her first marriage. That marriage ended with the death of the wife’s then husband, Mr E, in 1990. The second respondent was born in 1971 and is thus 54 years old. He has two adult children and a child aged 14 years and receives a Superannuation part-pension.
The applicant husband initiated proceedings on 9 July 2021. On 5 May 2022 he was given leave to proceed out of time.
The matter has been a fiercely fought property battle, where the wife had maintained, until the last day of trial, that a property acquired in her sole name in June 2022 (“the [Suburb C] property”) was held on trust for the second respondent (“their trust case”). The second respondent was at one with his mother, the first respondent wife, about that claim.
The husband resisted their trust case.
The matter came on for trial on 8 April 2025, but the hearing went part heard on 9 April 2025, when the husband suffered some kind of chest pains/tightness in his hotel room that morning. He did attend Court but was taken from Court by ambulance to hospital. All parties agreed that the trial needed to be adjourned.
The trial resumed and concluded in October 2025. On the last day of trial, being day five, the wife and second respondent resiled from their trust case.

The applicant husband’s case in a nutshell

Over four years of litigation and five days of trial, the husband maintained that any property in the wife’s name was property within the meaning of s 79 of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth)[1] (“the Act”).
By the time of submissions, his case was the pool was almost $890,000, mainly comprised of a home in the wife’s name, with an agreed, but old value of $865,000.[2] He has a motor vehicle with an agreed value of $13,975 and superannuation of $4,336.
The husband sought a 50:50 adjustment.
But from his proposed 50:50 entitlement of about E$445,000 (including his car and superannuation), he must pay liabilities sitting outside the pool, being monies owed to his litigation lenders and lawyers of almost $270,000[3] and has other personal liabilities of almost $21,500.[4]
He lives in a boarding house and has lived in his car at times since separation.

The wife’s and second respondent’s cases (plural) in a nutshell

Since acquiring the Suburb C property in 2022, and until the morning of the fifth (and last) day of trial, the wife had maintained that whilst the Suburb C property was in her sole name, the beneficial interest was “100 per cent” the second respondent’s.
Thus, up to the morning of day five of trial (after she abandoned more than a $1,000,000 of misguided add-backs and post-separation and personal liabilities), her case was that the pool was, in practical reality, just over $23,000 – the husband’s car at almost $14,000 and his superannuation of almost $4,340 and her car with a nett equity of about $5,000.
She too had various personal liabilities of about $112,500[5] and potentially owes earlier lawyers another almost $50,000.
By the time of the second tranche of the trial in October 2025, she described herself as couch surfing.
As foreshadowed, at the start of submissions on the last day of trial, the wife and second respondent abandoned their trust case.
The wife confirmed in submissions on day five, that she now took the position that the property at Suburb C was hers both beneficially and legally. She said it was available to the pool, but that the Court would not make any adjustment either pursuant to Stanford,[6] or by the usual path of ss 79 and 75 of the Act if the Court was against her on Stanford.
She confirmed that after more than 20 years of marriage, where they had joint real property acquisitions, built a business together, used proceeds from that joint business endeavour to purchase other real estate in their own names, lived in each other’s properties, holidayed together, shared living expenses and so on, the husband ought to walk away with his car with an agreed value of $13,975, and his nominal superannuation in the sum of $4,336.
After the wife abandoned the trust case and finished her submissions, the second respondent was given time to consider his position[7] – to follow his mother and resile from their trust case, or, to continue to assert his beneficial interest.
When the matter returned to Court, the second respondent told the Court he would “fall in line with her case” (that is his mother, the first respondent) and no longer asserted he held the beneficial interest in the Suburb C property. He appropriately said he did not need to make any submissions about the matrimonial property dispute.

Material

The applicant husband relied on the following documents:
Amended Application for Final Orders filed 27 March 2025;
Affidavit of Mr Ludas filed 10 March 2025; and
Applicant’s financial statement filed 7 March 2025.
The first respondent wife relied on the following documents:[8]
Amended Response filed 6 October 2025;
Affidavit of Ms Zhivko filed 12 March 2025;
Affidavit of Mr F filed 20 May 2023; and
Financial Statement of Ms Zhivko filed 10 March 2025.
For completeness, the second respondent filed the following documents, but they became superfluous when he abandoned the trust case and he made no submission at the end of the trial:
Amended Response to Final Orders filed 2 October 2025;
Affidavit of Mr V Zhivko filed 17 March 2025; and
Financial Statement of Mr V Zhivko filed 9 March 2025.
The first respondent did not take the Court to anything in the second respondent’s material in support of her s 79 case when making submissions.
The parties also filed a joint balance sheet on 3 April 2025, which initially contained 35 items. Prior to its streamlining, the first respondent sought over $1,000,000 in addbacks against the husband, including items such as his draw down of superannuation, even though she had done the same. She also wanted to add back a post separation workers’ compensation payment which he applied to living and a personal injuries payment received by the husband, even though his personal injury lawyers took all and he received nothing in his hands.
Sense prevailed and the pool was whittled down to just five items by the time of submissions – the Suburb C property in the wife’s name, a vehicle each, a loan for the wife’s car and the husband’s meagre superannuation.
The affidavit of the single expert witness Nigel Welch filed 3 April 2025 was also read; he was the property valuer. His valuation report is dated 13 September 2024. He was not required for cross-examination.
On 9 April 2025, Orders were made for an updated valuation of the Suburb C property, but the second respondent told the Court several times thereafter that he was “unable” to comply. At earlier mentions and during the trial, the Court made it clear to all parties that depending on the findings made, and the orders that may follow, an option for the Court may be to order the Suburb C property be sold to ascertain its up-to-date value.
The applicant, first respondent and second respondent were cross-examined. I do not consider general findings about credit to assist. Rather, I will look at the issues in dispute.
Mr F was not required for cross-examination. His evidence was largely comprised of hearsay, gratuitous commentary and inflammatory conclusions such as the husband’s spending “addiction” and that the husband “needs help”. His affidavit attracts little weight. He also spoke to the husband and wife having had a property settlement, which is dealt with in other places in these Reasons.
As required by r 8.15(3) of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) (“the Rules”), various annexures to the parties’ and experts’ affidavits became Exhibits. In all, the parties tendered 19 Exhibits.

LEGAL PRINCIPLES

Section 140 of the Evidence Act 1995 (Cth) guides my fact finding.
It is well settled that it is not necessary for a trial judge, in reaching a decision, to refer to every piece of evidence or argument presented during the trial. In Whisprun Pty Ltd v Dixon,[9] Gleeson CJ, McHugh and Gummow JJ said this:
...A judge’s reasons are not required to mention every fact or argument relied on by the losing party as relevant to an issue. Judgments of trial judges would soon become longer than they already are if a judge’s failure to mention such facts and arguments would be evidence that he or she had not properly considered the losing party’s case.
In Housing Commission of New South Wales v Tatmar Pastoral Co Pty Ltd and Penrith Pastoral Co Pty Ltd,[10] Mahoney JA said this:
It is not the duty of the judge to decide every matter which is raised in argument.

...

Nor is it necessary for a judge who is exercising a discretionary judgment to detail each factor which he has found to be relevant or irrelevant, or to itemize, for example, in the assessment of damages for tort, each of the factual matters to which he has had regard ... Nor is a judge required to make an explicit finding on each disputed piece of evidence. It will be sufficient, if the inference as to what is found is appropriately clear...
As this trial commenced on 8 April 2025, it was common ground that the Act that stood at the time was the relevant law, and not the recent amendments to Part VIII of the Act.

Justice and equity

The question that arises is whether, pursuant to s 79(2) of the Act, it is just and equitable to make orders altering the property interests of the parties by identifying the existing legal and equitable property interests of the parties. It must not be assumed that the parties’ rights to or interests in property are or should be different from those that then exist or that a party has the right to have the parties’ property divided by reference to considerations set out in s 79(4) of the Act (see Stanford at [37]–[40] and [50]).
Stanford also made clear that “the just and equitable requirement” pursuant to s 79(2) of the Act should not be conflated with the requirements of s 79(4). That said, in Bevan[11] and more recently in Cosola,[12] the Appeal Division of this Court held that the separate consideration for the purposes of s 79(2) of the Act can take account of the matters requiring consideration in s 79(4), even if such consideration is not mandatory.
As said in Stanford at [36]:
The expression ‘just and equitable’ is a qualitative description of a conclusion reached after examination of a range of potentially competing considerations. It does not admit of exhaustive definition. It is not possible to chart its metes and bounds...
It is accepted there is no “community” in property owned by spouses individually and the question of whether it is just and equitable to make an order “is not to be answered by assuming that the parties’ rights or interests in marital property are or should be different” from those at the time when the discretion may be exercised.[13]
It is also accepted that unless property rights or interests are adjusted pursuant to s 79, one spouse has no inchoate or other proprietary interest in the exclusive property owned by the other spouse.[14]
The length of the parties’ relationship is not dispositive of whether it is just and equitable to make a s 79 order.[15] Similarly, if no order is made, the wife will retain $869,690 and the husband $18,311, but that disparity itself does not justify making a s 79 order.
In Chancellor, the trial judge held the decision to keep finances separate was irrelevant.[16] Rather, the parties conduct throughout their relationship was the issue. The appeal against the first instance orders was dismissed.
If, and only if, the Court is satisfied that it is just and equitable for the property interests of the parties to be altered, the process of evaluating the proper orders to make is dictated by the factors enumerated within s 79(4) of the Act. Hence, the Court must necessarily identify and assess the parties’ contributions within the meaning of ss 79(4)(a)–(c) and then take account of the relevant matters referred to in ss 79(4)(d)–(g) and 75(2).

THE BALANCE SHEET

The second respondent said he was “unable” to comply with the Order of 9 April 2025 for an updated valuation of the Suburb C property because the husband’s lawyers had lodged caveats over the property to secure litigation lending. He did not want any increase in value of the property being used to support more litigation lending to the husband. The wife joined the second respondent in that position.
However, upon the first and second respondents abandoning their trust case, all parties agreed an updated joint valuation was necessary and that the applicant husband and first respondent wife would supply that to chambers as soon as it was done. Although no longer a party with a claim in the property, the second respondent said he would facilitate same.
No updated valuation eventuated. I consider this further under the heading “Disposition and Orders”.
The balance sheet is then as follows, which also sets out who has the legal and beneficial interest in the items:
ASSETS

Ownership
	
Description
	
Agreed value

1
	
First Respondent
	
B Street, Suburb C QLD [...]
	
$865,000 as at 13 September 2024, but to be sold for Reasons given later

2
	
Applicant
	
Motor Vehicle 1
	
$13,975

3
	
First Respondent
	
Motor Vehicle 2
	
$17,200

LIABILITIES

4
	
First Respondent
	
J Bank Car loan
	
$12,510

SUPPERANNUATION

Member
	
Name of fund
	
Type of interest
	
Agreed value

5
	
Applicant
	
Super Fund 1
	
Accumulation
	
$4,336

	
NETT TOTAL ASSETS
	
$883,665

	
NETT TOTAL ASSETS

(including superannuation)

	
$888,001

IS IT JUST AND EQUITABLE TO MAKE AN ORDER UNDER S 79 OF THE ACT?

A gravamen of the wife’s case under Stanford is that as far as she was concerned, the husband and wife had their property settlement when the Suburb H property was sold in 2007,[17] and the proceeds distributed per the title – 60 percent to her and 40 percent to the husband. She submitted it would not be just and equitable to make a s 79 adjustive order.
The husband and wife bought this property in 1996 for $455,000 and it was initially held as tenants in common in equal shares. In late 1999 it was held as joint tenants. By May 2003, the title was changed to the 60:40 basis because the wife wanted recognition of her greater contributions to its purchase and a trip to Disneyland she funded for the husband and wife and his children.[18]
The parties also owned a block of land at Suburb K in Queensland, which they had acquired in 2001 for $200,000 as joint tenants. In September 2007 the wife had the husband transfer his interest to her for “natural love and affection”.[19] The husband said in an earlier affidavit that this was to reflect she brought more by way of assets to the relationship.[20] In his trial affidavit he said he transferred the property because the wife had told him it would not make sense for two people to own the land but the house only be in one name.[21] The wife said the property went into her name because that was more tax efficient for both of them.[22] I will put aside what the husband said in an earlier affidavit and prefer his evidence in his trial affidavit. Either way, they were working as an economic unit and making decisions about a jointly held property and sharing financial information.
Even if the wife thought they had had their property settlement in 2007, that does not bind this Court.[23] The husband was asked about this as follows:
Okay. And I suggest that the reason why the property was transferred was because, at that point, you were settling up your affairs between you and [Ms Zhivko], and you were separating out your properties, and that that was consideration for the contribution she has made during the relationship. Do you agree or disagree?---I disagree

(Transcript 8 April 2025, p.61, lines 29–32)
True, the husband signed a Statutory Declaration in February 2018 that they would not claim against each other,[24] but that too is not binding on the Court and it is clear in emails exchanged between the parties that the wife was pressuring the husband to yield to her requirements about property arrangements, even to the point of surreptitiously taping him, which she used in these proceedings.[25]
The reality though – even on the wife’s case – is that after 2007 to separation a decade later, they shared everyday living expenses such as food and expenses on social activities and holidays. [26]
But there was more to their shared economic operations than that.
For example, whilst the husband and wife did not jointly own real property together after 2007, they lived together and shared common use in a property at Suburb L bought in the husband’s sole name from the co-owned Suburb H property proceeds. They shared common use of that property from late 2007 to April 2015. The wife deposed she did not assist with the mortgage in the husband’s name.
When the shared business endeavour N Pty Ltd was sold in 2008 for almost $1,000,000 nett (with respect to their interests), they each used their respective shares of the sale proceeds to fund living expenses, go on joint holidays and the wife put monies toward the build on the Suburb K property (the property which had been in joint names and then transferred to the wife for no consideration). The parties eventually lived there together when the build was completed.
The wife had the husband extensively cross-examined on how he spent his share of the N Pty Ltd proceeds and how he generally spent money from June 2008 to June 2010[27] – after they had apparently separated their finances on her case. The entries include references to various mobile networks, Tax Office payments, ASIC, payments to numerous credit cards (which is how they seemed to live their lives, with the wife deposing to using lump sums to reduce credit card debt), various gas water and electricity providers, a newsagency, a pharmacy, a washing machine and so on. It is not clear what I was meant to make of any of this. As was said in Mayne & Mayne:[28]
It is not the Court’s function to conduct an audit of the marriage or of the relationship finances. The parties’ remedies for resolving disputes about expenditure while they are together are centred on them and them alone. Choosing one transaction from many prior to separation for different treatments, specifically “to be added-back” or notionally included in the pool of property may make doing justice and equity between the parties difficult.
From mid-2015 to October 2016 they then lived at the Suburb K property – a property initially jointly owned from 2001 but transferred solely to the wife in 2007 for “natural love and affection”. The husband made contributions to the furnishing of the house, including audio-visual equipment, but the wife met the mortgage.[29]
When the Suburb K property was sold, they moved together into a property at Suburb M which was bought in the names of the wife and second respondent. The husband and wife remained in that home until physical separation in February 2018. The two respondents complained about works the husband had undertaken on this property, which is tacit acknowledgment he made contributions to it, even if unnecessary or not wanted on their view. It also demonstrates a belief or state of mind about common usage of property, at least from the husband’s perspective.
The wife’s contention they had their property settlement in 2007 also overlooks the fact that in addition to sharing living expenses and homes, they went on four holidays together between 2008 to 2016, including what the husband called a second honeymoon in 2016 at a cost of about $80,000 largely funded by his credit card. The wife accepted he paid for business class flights, and they shared a cabin on a cruise.
For Christmas 2014, he also bought her a $13,000 necklace which she later sold for $10,000 to fund legal fees in these proceedings.
On the 2016 $80,000 trip, he bought her a sculpture in Europe, which the wife initially wanted included in her chattels in the pool, being part of Item 11, and spoke of the statue being “subject to valuation”. She eventually gave up on that (the parties agreed to removed their de minimis contents from the pool).
They opened a joint account in 2017 too, although the wife neither credited or debited money to the account; it was so she could help the husband budget. They also shared a bedroom to September 2017 on her case.[30]
Throughout their cohabitation they operated on largely traditional gendered homemaker arrangements – he looked after the outside of the home and she the inside. At one point the wife did fund a gardener and pool person, but on the whole, each pulled their weight to the upkeep of various homes in which they lived and to homelife.
The wife submitted that it was an unstated assumption between the parties from 2007 that they would deal with their own properties separately, but that is a narrow focus on real properties and not a focus on the parties’ conduct. That submission is undercut by the reality that for a decade after 2007 they enjoyed common usage of properties irrespective of the title, accumulated joint assets such as chattels as the wife acknowledged in her 2018 emails to the husband, continued with their respective homemaker contributions, and shared living and social expenses and went on joint holidays.
The wife also relied upon Cosola. Whilst there are some factual similarities to the current case, there are others which distinguish it – although each case must turn on its own facts and circumstances. In Cosola, the Appeal Division recorded the following:
The parties did not operate or share a personal joint bank account during their relationship (at [71]). They did not acquire real estate jointly, nor did they obtain any joint loans (at [61] and [77]). There was no evidence before the primary judge that the parties discussed how they would manage their financial affairs (at [76]), that they had made future plans for their life together, or that they had discussed how they would share their property if they retired or separated (at [78]).
Here, and by reference to the applicant’s and first respondent’s conduct during their relationship from 1996 to separation in late 2017/early 2018, I find:
(a) The parties did have a joint bank account at least in 2017, although I accept the wife did not transact on it but helped the husband’s financial planning;

(b) They did jointly acquire real estate being the property at Suburb H and land at Suburb K;

(c) They did acquire joint mortgages for both the Suburb H property and Suburb K land;

(d) They did discuss their financial affairs – for example, on the wife’s case, the husband’s accountant told them both that the husband ought transfer his interest in the Suburb K land to the wife for effective tax planning for both of them; [31]

(e) The husband transferred his interest in the Suburb K land to the wife for no consideration, but natural love and affection (albeit relief thereafter from the ensuing mortgage);

(f) The wife certainly instigated discussions in at least 2007 and 2018 about how their property would be sorted;

(g) Their shared endeavour developing a business (N Pty Ltd) of which the husband was the keyman and prime mover in its operations;

(h) Subsequent property acquisitions primarily from the proceeds of the co-owned Suburb H property and sale of the N Pty Ltd business;

(i) Living together and sharing the common use of homes after the sale of the Suburb H property, first in a house in the husband’s name from 2007 to 2015 (largely funded per (h) just above), and then in a house in the wife’s name from 2015 to 2016, and then in a house in the wife and second respondent’s names from 2016 to early 2018 (with funds which can be traced back to (h) above);

(j) From 2007, as before, they shared everyday living expenses such as food and expenses on social activities and holidays;[32]

(k) The myriad of contributions each made during the length of the relationship as set out under the contributions heading below;

(l) The fact of separation means they no longer share a mutuality of agreed living arrangements that had been “sufficient for the purposes of that husband and wife during the continuance of their marriage” and there is not thereafter “the common use of property by the husband and wife” (emphasis added).[33] To that end, the Appeal Division of this Court in Oamra said:

Counsel for the wife submitted that to the extent that the primary judge relied upon there having been some common “use” of property that must be only a reference to the common use of the former matrimonial home. Even if that was so, it is unclear why that is not a significant finding in the consideration of s 79(2) of the Act and relevant to the question as to whether any order should be made. Counsel for the wife argues that in almost all marriages and de facto relationships, each spouse will inevitably and mutually confer benefits on the other, and further argues that the reference to “common use” at [42] of Stanford should be understood to refer not to the fact that there has been “use” which would exist in almost every marriage or de facto relationship where a home is shared, but to a legal or equitable entitlement to use property. We do not accept that the High Court’s use of the word “use” should be limited in that way. In fact, the opening words of [42], namely, “[i]n many cases where an application is made for a property settlement order, the just and equitable requirement is readily satisfied...” must contemplate cases where during a long relationship a party has made significant contributions under s 79(4)(c) of the Act but has no legal or equitable right to use property which the parties have in fact been using throughout that relationship.
(Emphasis added)
(m) The husband’s payment of private health insurance and funeral insurance for both to April and June 2018 respectively. He also met her O Company Roadside Assistance.[34] The wife’s Separation Declaration to Centrelink, signed 13 November 2017 confirmed the husband paid “joint private medical insurance”;[35](n) In June 2018, the husband emailed the wife that he would change his Will to “deliberately exclude” her and the second respondent as beneficiaries;[36]

(o) The wife prepared a new Letter of Wishes in March 2017 where she asked the second respondent to grant the husband a three to six month tenancy at the Suburb M property to give him time to find another place of residence upon her demise and she subsequently told Centrelink that the husband was a beneficiary under her Will;[37]

(p) In an email of 1 April 2018, the wife acknowledged they had “goods and chattels purchased as a couple over the 20 years we have been married” in the “day to day running of a household” which are not “owned by you or me but collectively”.[38] Apart from highlighting the mutuality of their financial arrangements, this runs counter to the wife’s notion of strict financial separation from 2007; and

(q) At trial in October 2025, the second respondent said the first respondent wife had “boxes and boxes” [39] of the husband’s and wife’s financial records at her home, hardly showing conduct of separate financial affairs.

I conclude, for any combination of, or for the accumulation of the matters just listed, that it is just and equitable to make a property order under s 79 of the Act.

CONTRIBUTIONS

As the Court is satisfied that it is just and equitable for the property interests of the parties to be altered, evaluating the proper orders to make is dictated by the factors enumerated within s 79(4) of the Act. Hence, the Court must necessarily identify and assess the husband’s and wife’s contributions within the meaning of ss 79(4)(a)–(c) and then take account of the relevant matters referred to in ss 79(4)(d)–(g) and 75(2).
The applicant husband proposed that contributions be assessed equally, pointing to the length of the relationship and how they operated as an economic unit over those many years. Or to paraphrase what Deane J (as he was then) said in Mallet,[40] “their marriage constituted a practical union of both lives and property”. It must be observed however that despite the length of the relationship, there is no presumption of equality of contributions as a starting point.
If I was against the wife on her Stanford argument (which I am) the wife proposed that contributions be assessed almost entirely in her favour and the husband ought walk away with his vehicle at $13,975 and superannuation of $4,336.
The wife’s case very much focused on her direct financial contribution of two properties at the start of the relationship, which can be traced through to subsequent acquisitions of property including the current Suburb C property. However, as explained in Dickons,[41] contributions are assessed from a much broader church than that:
...it is self-evident that financial contributions (whether direct or indirect) can be made to a relationship that have an effect on the property of the parties without those financial contributions finding their way directly into, or being directly linked to, specific property or, indeed, directly to the totality of the property available for distribution at the time of trial. Financial contributions can be made to the “...acquisition, conservation or improvement...” of property “...directly or indirectly...” (s 79(4)(a). Emphasis added). A financial contribution can be made indirectly by, for example, the use by parties of income or assets for purpose A freeing up the use of other income or assets for purpose B. Moreover, a particular financial contribution might have been used wholly in discretionary expenditure which, but for that contribution, would not have been available to the parties or would have required borrowings or a diminution of capital. Such a contribution can also, in that way, be seen, for example, as an indirect contribution to the conservation of property. Indeed, the principles discussed for example in In the Marriage of Kowaliw [1981] FamCA 70; (1981) FLC 91-092 and In the Marriage of Townsend [1994] FamCA 144; (1995) FLC 92-569, can be seen as an exception to that general proposition.
... Any and all such contributions, whether or not they sound in, or are directly linked to, the property available for distribution, should be considered and assessed together with the nature, form and extent of all other contributions of all types contemplated otherwise by s 79(4).
... s 79(4) specifically requires the Court to take into account contributions made to the welfare of the family (and substantively and “...not in any merely token way...”; see, Mallett v Mallett [1984] HCA 21; (1984) 156 CLR 605 at 636 per Wilson J) notwithstanding that those contributions may not be, or cannot be seen to be, directly linked to the available property at trial, or any increase or decrease in the value of the property.
Put another way, consistent with authority, the s 79 discretion involves as a necessary requirement that “... trial Judges weigh and assess the contributions of all kinds and from all sources made by each of the parties throughout the period of their cohabitation and then translate such an assessment into a percentage of the overall property of the parties or provide for a transfer of property in specie in accordance with that assessment.” (In the Marriage of Aleksovski [1996] FamCA 111; (1996) FLC 92-705 at 83,437....
Ultimately, the assessment of contributions must take an holistic approach.[42] I nevertheless use temporal sub-headings in the discussion that follows simply to signpost what was being contributed by each at the start, during their relationship and post-separation.

Section 79(4)(a)–(c)

Initial contributions

At the commencement of the parties’ cohabitation in 1996, the husband had been living in rental accommodation in New South Wales. He had a motor vehicle, savings of approximately $10,000, and an unknown amount of superannuation, which he accepted in cross-examination was “nominal”.
On the other hand, the wife owned two properties: an unencumbered property at Suburb P, New South Wales (the former matrimonial home with her deceased husband) which had some form of bank valuation of $260,000; and a property at Suburb Q, New South Wales said to be worth $240,000 on the same bank valuation. The bank document listed a debt to R Bank of $75,000.[43] The first respondent also took out a loan of $30,000 to meet the short fall between available funds from her first husband’s death benefit and the purchase price of Suburb Q. It is not clear if the wife’s $30,000 is part of the $75,000 listed on the bank document, or not. In any event, the second respondent was required to pay back his mother the $30,000 over time.[44] He did.[45]
The basis for these values seems to be the bald assertions in bank documents,[46] but with the foundation for those opinions unknown.[47] Nevertheless, the husband accepted these amounts in cross-examination, and it is uncontroversial that the wife’s two properties allowed the parties to buy a property at Suburb H in June 1996 for $455,000.[48] The applicant and first respondent resided there from 1996 to 2007.
The wife also had superannuation of $200,000.
In her affidavit, the wife said the second respondent beneficially owned the Suburb Q property, which if true, would reduce her initial contribution. However, that is a red herring; even if the second respondent did have some form of beneficial interest (which the husband accepted in cross-examination)[49] the wife nevertheless pledged her son’s apparent equity in the Suburb Q home to the bank (along with her Suburb P property) as security for the husband’s and wife’s purchase of the Suburb H property.
The husband said he contributed approximately $35,000 to $40,000 towards expenses relating to the fit-out of the Suburb H property. The wife is silent on this in her affidavit. I do not accept the quantum provided by the husband as it is not clear how he could source the sum. Yet, I do accept he did some works on the property as that is consistent with his latter actions in other homes.
On any assessment, the wife’s initial contributions completely overshadowed the husband’s, and I find accordingly. But it is not a matter of holding that contribution up for measurement against all other contributions.[50] Rather, the myriad of contributions over the length of the relationship and post-separation must be assessed holistically.[51]

During the relationship

The wife’s Suburb Q property was sold in August 1996 for $242,000 and $214,500 was applied to the Suburb H mortgage.[52] The wife said, and I accept, she and the husband agreed to the sale.[53]
In April 1997, the parties jointly entered a hire purchase agreement for a $79,000 car for the husband. He traded in his car he brought to the relationship for almost $20,000 to reduce indebtedness; $56,750 was borrowed. The husband took over the payments once he could draw a wage from the N Pty Ltd business referred to in the next paragraph.
In May 1997, the husband and wife joined with third parties to establish a business that became, what can be conveniently called, N Pty Ltd. The parties refinanced the Suburb H property and used $60,000 for the business. The husband also said he applied some superannuation to the start-up but accepted in cross-examination that his superannuation was “nominal” in value.
The husband and wife shared a 50 percent interest in the business. The husband worked in the business full-time, being 50-60 hours a week. The wife helped with administrative tasks outside of her full-time remunerative employment with S Company.
Begrudgingly, the wife conceded in cross-examination the husband developed the N Pty Ltd business and spent more time than her working there. I accept the submission he was the “prime mover” of developing the business which eventually sold for just under $1,000,000 nett with respect to their interests. The husband earned between $4,000 to $10,000 a week. The wife was earning in the vicinity of $140,000 to $150,000 as a senior executive with S Company until she was made redundant in 2004.
The parties married in November 1997.
In July 1998, the wife’s Suburb P property was sold for $255,0000, from which the wife received almost $254,000. From the proceeds, they spent $30,000 on a holiday to Disneyland with the husband’s children, installed air conditioning for the Suburb H property, bought custom made furniture of almost $45,000 (the available invoices are in both names),[54] a $7,500 pool table (invoice in wife’s name)[55] and $25,000 towards the installation of a shed on the Suburb H property. The Development Approval for the shed is in the names of both the husband and wife.[56]
The wife said the remainder of the Suburb P property sale proceeds were used “to assist in the establishment of our business ‘[N Pty Ltd]’ and on general living expenses”. I accept expenditure on general living as that is consistent with the pattern of the wife (and husband) receiving money and using it for living, but the assertion about money to N Pty Ltd is vague and gives no sense of quantum or purpose.
The wife also received a retention bonus of $60,000 gross in 1998[57] which she applied to general living expenses.
During the parties’ ownership of the Suburb H property, the title was changed several times. It was initially acquired as tenants in common in equal shares. In October 1999, the title was changed to a joint tenancy as between the husband and wife. On 22 May 2003, the title was amended to a 60:40 split favouring the wife to take account of her initial contribution of the Suburb P property and the money she spent on the trip with the husband and his two children to Disneyland.[58]
In May 2001, the parties purchased a block of land in joint names at Suburb K. It was bought for $200,000 entirely funded by a joint mortgage.
In March 2004 the wife was made redundant from S Company and received $231,830 (nett).[59] A few days later in April 2004 she received $354,642 as a pension/superannuation payment.[60]
From the redundancy and pension/superannuation, the wife said the sums were applied to general living, a holiday to America (not the Disneyland one with his children), and clearing some accumulated credit card debt.[61] The wife submitted in her affidavit that when she paid off credit card debt that was for both their benefits,[62] but when the husband did so, it was only of benefit to him.[63] Given she deposed several times to sharing of general living expenses, social activities, and the like, her submission is hard to maintain. It is but one of several examples where the wife would claim credit for her actions but be critical of the husband for doing the same.
She also bought a car for the husband’s son for $19,500 but was repaid this when the husband received an inheritance of $60,000 from his mother’s estate in 2005/2006.[64] The husband said he gave her $30,000, not just the car money.
The wife then had 12 months off remunerative work outside the home; she oversaw the Suburb H property works, assisted in N Pty Ltd and she and the husband went to the USA.
In May 2005 the wife commenced a new job on $60,000 per annum.
The Suburb K land remained in their joint names with a joint mortgage from 2001 to late September 2007, when the husband transferred his interest to the wife. There was no monetary consideration to him for this with the transfer document showing it was transferred for “natural love and affection from between [sic] a husband and wife.”[65]
At the time of the transfer, the wife took over the previously joint mortgage, acquired a further loan in her name to build on the land, and applied some of the N Pty Ltd proceeds for that purpose as well. The parties had not paid anything much, if at all, off the Suburb K mortgage by the time of the transfer in 2007; it stood at $207,408.50 including transfer costs.[66] The wife increased the mortgage to a total of $440,000[67] to facilitate the build and applied almost $458,000 she received from the Suburb H property proceeds and $242,500 from the sale of N Pty Ltd to same,[68] which had both been co-owned assets. He also said they would go together to inspect the building progress. The wife did not dispute this.
In an earlier affidavit, the husband said he transferred his interest in the Suburb K property to the wife to reflect her initial contributions;[69] but did not repeat that in his trial affidavit. Instead, the husband deposed in his trial affidavit that he transferred the property because the wife had told him it would not make sense for two people to own the land but the house only be in one name.[70] The wife said the property went into her name because that was more tax efficient for both of them.[71] I put aside what he said in an earlier affidavit and rely on his trial material. On either the husband’s trial position or wife’s case, they were working as an economic unit and making decisions about a jointly held property and sharing financial information.
In December 2007, the wife entered into a contract to build on the Suburb K land. The contract price for the build was $608,500 and the contract was in the wife’s name.[72] Variations of $171,426 were made taking the build total to $779,926.[73]
The husband said he directly contributed money for the Suburb K property in the form of building works and custom furnishings.[74] In cross-examination the premise of a question to the husband was that he “made modest improvements to the property by way of adding things like an audio-visual asset?”. The husband agreed.[75]
In October 2007, the parties sold the Suburb H property for a gross of $1,220,000. Of the proceeds of almost $763,000, the husband received just over $305,000 and the wife almost $458,000.[76] This is when the wife says they had their property settlement,[77] even though they subsequently lived in and shared common use of each other’s subsequently purchased homes for another decade, which can be traced back to matrimonial assets (the Suburb H property and N Pty Ltd) and continued to share everyday living expenses such as “food and expenses on social activities and holidays”.[78]
I have already rejected the notion of a financial settlement in 2007 and given reasons for that.
After the sale of Suburb H, the applicant and first respondent then moved into a property at Suburb L, New South Wales (“the [Suburb L] property”), which the husband purchased in late October 2007 for $655,000 with $246,498 from the Suburb H property proceeds and a mortgage of just over $376,000.[79] The husband said he bought the property at the wife’s insistence. The applicant and first respondent lived in this property exercising common usage despite the legal and equitable title being in the husband’s sole name, from 2007 until April 2015 and continued to share living expenses, groceries and social activities.[80]
The husband solely paid the Suburb L mortgage, and the wife serviced the Suburb K mortgage.
In late 2008, N Pty Ltd was sold for just over $2,246,000 gross[81] and almost $1,977,000 nett.[82] The wife said that under the shareholder arrangements with third parties, the husband and wife ought have equally shared in 50 per cent of the proceeds, being $988,447. The husband said he received E$495,000[83] and the wife $242,500.[84] Proceeds were applied by her to the Suburb K build, by him to the Suburb L mortgage, and by both of them to savings, living and joint holidays.
The wife said she ought have received an additional E$242,500 from the sale but did not. The 2010 liquidator’s first and final distribution of assets records the husband received $741,328 and the wife $247,109.[85] I prefer the liquidator’s record over the husband’s inability to explain what happened with the difference between the $495,000 he said he received, and the sum recorded by the liquidator.
The husband stayed working in the N Pty Ltd business for the new owners and was paid $135,000-$140,000 gross per annum. He received a redundancy payment in 2017.
The build on the Suburb K, Queensland, property was finished in December 2008. The second respondent lived there from 2009 to 2015 and then retained a room at the property until it was sold in 2016.[86] It does not seem he paid rent whilst residing there. The applicant and first respondent were still living in the Suburb L property in New South Wales. The wife moved out of the property in March 2012 but moved back in in July 2012.
In July 2009, the wife drew down on the last of her superannuation being $18,712.[87] She applied this to general living expenses.[88]
In early 2015, the husband rolled over his superannuation of $103,897[89] to a pension account. There is no evidence to support a finding he used this for his sole benefit. Rather, the wife’s evidence is they shared household expenses and the like.
The Suburb L property was sold in April 2015 for $1,1500,000 with a nett of just over $511,000.[90] The husband then acquired a property at Suburb T, Queensland for $530,000 in September 2015, with a mortgage of $408,000[91] and some of the proceeds of the Suburb L property sale.[92] This property was rented and the rent applied to the mortgage. The wife managed “on behalf of [Mr Ludas], the records for the income and expenditure related to the rental and expenses of the property for a period of time”.[93]
After the 2015 sale of the Suburb L property, the parties moved together into the Suburb K property, which by now was in the wife’s name. She had been servicing the mortgage since its transfer to her sole name in 2007, but had been living rent/mortgage free in the Suburb L home. At the Suburb K property, the parties continued to share household expenses, food, groceries and entertainment, and the husband also made some monetary contributions to the wife whilst they lived there.[94]
It is agreed that the husband paid for a TV or two and other AV equipment for the Suburb K property and then for the Suburb M property, but the two respondents said he took that equipment with him. The husband said they were all sold with the home as fixtures. Exhibit 2, e-p.160 paragraph 2 is part of an email from the husband to the wife dated 9 March 2018. It leans more to one of the TVs and AV equipment being taken by the husband post-separation, and I find accordingly, but I accept and find another TV (perhaps in the lounge) and speakers mounted into the ceiling were fixtures and remained with the home. Little turns on this in any event.
In May 2016, the husband funded a one-month $80,000 overseas trip for the husband and wife, which he called their second honeymoon. The wife lost her job after this, and the husband offered to pay the Suburb K mortgage.[95] She received a payment of $10,187 from her employer.[96]
The Suburb K property was sold in late 2016 for $1,195,000 with the wife receiving, on her case, a nett of just over $718,000.[97] The wife said she applied the proceeds to the purchase of a property at Suburb M for a total of $659,652 (land bought in one transaction and home in a separate contract) in joint names with the second respondent.
Confusingly, the second respondent said his mother received $506,773 and he received $657,687, but after discharge of the mortgage in the first respondent’s name she really only received $70,828.[98]
Despite whatever went on with the two respondents in terms of finances, it is clear the sale of the Suburb K property (which had been previously owed by the husband and wife as joint tenants) went towards the purchase of the Suburb M property in the joint names of the first and second respondents.
The second respondent deposed at his paragraph 334 that after the sale of the Suburb K property in late 2016, the wife also used $41,600 of the proceeds towards paying out her credit cards and gave $24,000 to the second respondent so he could do the same with his credit card. I accept that to be so, as it fits with the wife’s pattern of clearing credit cards when lump sums of money came her way.
The husband and wife moved into the Suburb M property on its settlement in late 2016.[99] The parties resided there together, until the husband left in February 2018.
After moving to Suburb M, the husband and wife opened a joint account in 2017. It is accepted by all that the wife did not transact on that account but helped the husband manage his spending, credit cards and “administration”, whatever that may mean. The husband also made monetary contributions to the wife of between $500 to $1,000 on an ad hoc basis.[100]
The husband received a redundancy payment in September 2017 of $32,000 which he applied to their general living expenses. In late 2017 he also drew on his superannuation account to pay credit card debts and living expenses.
The husband said he applied $10,000 for works on the Suburb M property.[101] Both respondents complained about the works he did[102] – which is tacit acceptance by them that he did. For example, he built a desk in a room to use as an office. The second respondent annexed various invoices in the husband’s name for work on the property.[103] Even if they were not in agreement about his works, the husband’s efforts to do so show, at least in his mind, a common use of the property and contributions made by him.
The second respondent and his family lived with the parties in the Suburb M property, from early 2018.
The husband also maintained private health insurance and funeral insurance for both of them to 30 April and 30 June 2018 respectively.[104] He met her O Company Roadside Assistance.[105]
During the course of the parties’ relationship, both the parties were employed for a majority of the time and earned salaries. It is plain both parties had significant amounts of monies pass through their hands between 1996 to late 2017/early 2018, from which they paid down accumulated significant credit card debt – noting they shared living and socialising costs.

Kennon[106]

I accept there was an instance of family violence where the husband acknowledged he pushed the wife, and she slipped on the tiled porch before she left to call the police from a neighbour’s home in 2002.[107] The husband was arrested and taken to a police station for the processing of a Temporary Intervention Order. No charges were preferred.[108]
This was an inexcusable occurrence by the applicant.
The wife was keen to impress that the husband was a violent, controlling man throughout the relationship, and had stalked her post-separation. It turned out in cross-examination that she had heard he was asking some unnamed person/s about her and did not know if he had ever gone close to the house, but that someone had seen him.
She said he had been “ordered” from the Suburb M house in February 2018 when the police were called to attend an argument. He was never ordered by court order from the house. In her affidavit she also deposed that when the police were called to the Suburb M home in February 2018:[109]
...[Mr Ludas] pushed [Mr V Zhivko’s] daughter [U] who was 7 years old at the time into a window, before pushing past me to get to [Mr V Zhivko]. He then lifted up a dining room chair and lifted it above his head threatening to hit [Mr V Zhivko] with it on his head...
The police report of that date says nothing of this kind.[110] The husband is recorded telling the police he and the second respondent had a heated argument about who could watch television in the loungeroom. He is recorded as saying “but there was nothing physical”.
The second respondent is recorded saying this, with no reference to an assault on the 7 year old and he, the second respondent adult son, was the one who did the pushing:
... his version is as follows: I moved up to find work in Queensland and am currently staying at my mum’s house. I heard the respondent yelling with my Mum and I told him to stop so it didn’t occur in front of my 7-year-old daughter. There was a verbal argument and it became heated. I pushed the respondent [the husband] away from me [adult son] as he was in my personal space. My Wife has then called Police.[111]

(emphasis added)
The police notes add:
The witnesses gave the same version. The aggrieved stated that he is not scared and doesn’t need Police protection. The respondent is going to bed and stated that on Wednesday he is going to Sydney for 5 days. There has been no history of domestic violence. There is [sic] no physical injuries. The aggrieved and respondent won’t be living together much longer so there is no need to protect the aggrieved.

Other Action or No DV: The aggrieved doesnt [sic] fear the respondent and the aggrieved is only there temporarily until he finds a job.
Had the husband assaulted the seven year old, and/or threatened the second respondent with a chair, then it beggars belief that the first and second respondent and his partner, who called police, said nothing about such a serious matter to the authorities.
It is not my conclusion that the wife was actively lying about allegations made in her affidavit just extracted above. Rather, her hatred of the husband and extremely negative view of him was redolent and palpable during the trial and earlier mentions.
For example, she applied for his lawyers to be referred to the Court’s Marshall for contempt[112] and applied for them to be recalled to the court for misleadingly saying (on her case) the applicant was not aware the parties were divorced and that the applicant’s Financial Statement was complete.[113]
For example, she expressed great bitterness and was critical of the husband’s character because he had been married thrice before her (thus she was wife four). She then feigned not knowing how many times her son, the second respondent, had been married – three times.
For example, she was ordered to pay almost $10,000 in costs to the husband for the filing of two doomed Applications in a Proceeding. Yet in cross-examination she maintained the costs order against her and in the husband’s favour was a “win” for her.
These are the glasses through which the wife perceives the husband. In turn, that enmity coloured her evidence about the husband and her dealings with him.
The wife also said the Suburb C property was in her sole name for her “safety” from the applicant husband, when insisting up to day five of trial that the adult son was the true beneficial owner. That meant her residence was easily searchable. Neither respondent could adequately explain that bizarre state of affairs other than their former lawyer told them to put the property in the wife’s name.
At the end of the day, no submission was made by the wife for a Kennon contribution (or s 75(2)(o)), adjustment to her.

Post-separation

The wife contended that the parties separated sometime in September 2017 although remained living under one roof to February 2018. She relied upon documents completed by the husband to Centrelink about separating in late 2017 – he said he did this to fast-track access to a pension on advice of the wife.
In November 2017, the wife notified Centrelink they had separated under one roof in September 2017.
The husband said they were not, in fact, separated until he left the Suburb M home in February 2018 after an argument broke out and the police were called as referred to under the Kennon heading just above. The husband left the house a few days later on [...] February 2018 and went to Sydney for a pre-planned business trip.
The second respondent ventured his views on separation, deposing: [114]
... the Applicant was and still is fully aware, that the relationship between the Applicant and the First Respondent irretrievably broke down on [...] February 2018, the day after the domestic violence incident on the night of the [...] February 2018.
The First Respondent told the Second Respondent, words to the effect “[Mr Ludas] and my marriage irretrievably broke down because of his action on that night against us but especially [U] (the Second Respondent’s daughter)”.
At the end of the day, nothing turns on whether final separation occurred late 2017 or early 2018.
At physical separation, the husband had credit card debts of $90,000 to $100,000. There is no evidence before me that that was expenditure solely for himself. Rather, he used credit cards to pay for the second honeymoon in 2016, a necklace as a gift to the wife (which she later sold for legal fees) and their general living expenses. He went on a debt consolidation program, sold various chattels and had some of the liabilities forgiven on hardship grounds.
In May 2018, the husband suffered a mental health episode and was hospitalised. Following this, he was repatriated to Sydney, where he commenced living with his brother. Then he lived in a variety of rental properties in Sydney, as well as periods of homelessness in which he resided in his car.
The husband sold the Suburb T property in early 2019, being post-separation, for $560,000. He said he used the proceeds of just over $71,500 on a rental property and general living. As he had had few other means of support, I accept that to be so.
The husband filed these proceedings on 9 July 2021. The wife then made enquiries about the husband’s assets and liabilities,[115] which is a curious thing to do for someone who believed they had twice affected a property settlement (first in 2007 on the sale of the Suburb H property and again in post-separation discussions in 2018).
The wife continued to live in the Suburb M property with the second respondent (and his family) until the time of its sale in early 2022 for $890,000, netting around $860,000.[116] The sale of the Suburb M property occurred after the commencement of these proceedings and without prior notice to the husband.
The wife then bought the Suburb C property in June 2022 for $755,000 in her sole name. The proceeds from the Suburb M property sale were applied to the purchase of the Suburb C property and the balance used for some of the wife’s legal fees in these proceedings, a mower and some works on the property.
Upon the Suburb C property purchase, and after proceedings had been instituted by the husband, the first and second respondents entered into a Deed to the effect that property bought in the wife’s sole name was held on trust for the second respondent; it did not specify the Suburb C property, but just “property”.[117] The Deed, dated 1 May 2022, is not worth the paper it is written on.[118] On the last day of trial, the first and second respondents abandoned their trust case that the property was beneficially owned by the second respondent thus the purported Deed falls away.
The first respondent and second respondent and his family, moved into the Suburb C property. The second respondent and his family remained living in the Suburb C property by the time of submissions in October 2025 when the wife described herself as couch surfing.
There is no suggestion that the husband made any direct financial contribution to the acquisition, maintenance or conservation of the Suburb C property. However, some of the proceeds of the sale of the co-owned Suburb H property and the N Pty Ltd business in 2008 found its way into the build on the Suburb K land (the land was held as joint tenants from 2001 to 2007 and by the wife thereafter), the sale proceeds of which were then applied by the wife to the purchase of the Suburb M property in 2016 (in which both the applicant and first respondent resided) and then eventually to the purchase of the Suburb C property in 2022.
On 2 July 2022, the first respondent borrowed almost $18,000 from the second respondent and they executed a loan agreement.[119] Prior to that on 9 April 2022, they entered into a rather odd “Personal Loan Pre-Approval Agreement”[120] - odd for the mother and adult son’s private arrangement. The wife deposed she used the monies for the costs of her move from Suburb M to Suburb C, legal fees and conveyancing costs and repayment of a loan from her sister-in-law of $5,000.[121] She is still repaying that to the second respondent, her adult son, and owed him $10,130 at the time of her trial affidavit. She is also solely paying a loan to fit solar panels to the Suburb M property even though the property was in the names of the first and second respondents.

A holistic assessment

In looking at contributions, it would be too simplistic and erroneous to concentrate on the wife’s Suburb C property because it constitutes the bulk of the pool. That is because contributions made by parties need not relate directly to the property they own at the time of trial. Indeed, property adjustment orders must be made with respect to the parties’ interests in the existing property so as to accommodate all of their contributions, whether made before, during, or after the relationship, and furthermore, the orders must be just and equitable by reference to such contributions.[122] Consequently, and for illustration purposes, a large financial contribution by one party to existing property may be outflanked by contributions of a quite different type made by the other party, but all of their contributions need to be reflected in their eventual entitlements to the existing property.[123]
Accordingly, it is accepted, and I find, that the Suburb C property is the most significant item in the property pool. It is also accepted, and I find, that its acquisition can be traced back to the wife’s overwhelming initial contribution of the Suburb Q and Suburb P properties to facilitate the purchase of the co-owned matrimonial home at Suburb H, and in turn, those sale proceeds can be traced to assisting with the purchases and sales of the Suburb K property, the Suburb M property and now the Suburb C property.
However, I also find that a focus on just tracing the wife’s initial contributions to the Suburb C property would not accommodate the various contributions made by the parties along that journey. Those various contributions are, and I find:
(a) Although acknowledging the wife helped with administrative tasks with N Pty Ltd, the husband was the “prime mover” in developing N Pty Ltd, which was then sold for almost $1 million nett for the husband’s and wife’s interest;

(b) The proceeds of the sale of the N Pty Ltd business and sale of the Suburb H property were applied to the Suburb L property and some to the build on the Suburb K land;

(c) The husband transferring his interest in the Suburb K property to the wife for no consideration, although relief from the mortgage which the wife met thereafter;

(d) The indirect contributions of the husband and wife each living in properties in the other’s name – both living in the Suburb L property in the husband’s name from 2007 to 2015 and then the Suburb K property and Suburb M property referable to the wife from 2015 to 2018. Obviously, they lived longer at the Suburb L property than the Suburb K and Suburb M property;

(e) Each servicing mortgages for the properties in their sole names whilst living with the other spouse;

(f) Each applying superannuation, redundancies and other lump sums for general living, accumulated credit card debts and joint holidays;

(g) The wife contributing her name to the hire purchase of a car, whilst the husband contributed his previous car as a trade in and then took over the debt when earning a wage with N Pty Ltd;

(h) The husband’s modest contributions to the fit outs at properties in Suburb H, Suburb K and Suburb M;

(i) The wife managing the rental of the Suburb T property for a period and helping the husband with some budgeting in 2017;

(j) Their respective and shared homemaker contributions;

(k) The sharing of general living expenses and costs of social activities;

(l) Each funding various shared holidays and the husband funding the purchase of a statute (which the wife wanted valued for inclusion in the pool until the last day of hearing) and buying her a necklace (which has gone to legal fees for the wife); and

(m) As the wife acknowledged they had purchased goods and chattels “as a couple over the 20 years we have been married” which are not “owned by you or me but collectively”.

Standing back and taking a holistic approach to all their various contributions, I assess contributions favour the wife 70:30.

Sections 79(4)(d)–(g) and 75(2)

The proposed orders will have no effect on the earning capacity of either party; s 79(4)(d). Sections 79(4)(f) and (g) are not relevant.
Section 79(4)(e) brings attention to s 75(2) of the Act in so far as those factors are relevant. Neither party sought an adjustment in their favour under s 75(2).
The parties are both in their 70s and have some health issues. Neither has capacity for much by way of gainful employment. By the time of the October 2025 part of the trial, the wife had left her home at Suburb C and was couch surfing and the husband was residing in a boarding house.
The husband is not currently employed and receives an aged pension of about $860 per fortnight. The wife too is in receipt of an aged pension of $1,044.40 per fortnight.
Both the applicant and first respondent have personal liabilities which sit outside the asset pool and which will, in reality, reduce their respective entitlements.
In the balance sheet, the wife included the husband’s loan from a litigation funder of approximately $180,000 as a financial resource – this is money he owes, not due to him. It is hardly a financial resource. In any event, save for the Robb & Robb[124] issue, which is addressed next, the wife proposed no s 75(2) adjustment to either party.

Robb & Robb

The husband’s children from an earlier relationship spent alternate weekends and holiday times with the husband and wife. Accordingly, the wife sought a Robb & Robb s 79(4) contributions adjustment in her favour. However, as set out in Alston & Alston:[125]
The authorities are quite clear that such a contribution, in assisting in the care of a non-biological child, should be assessed as a factor of potential relevance under s 75(2)(o) of the Act, rather than as contributions made pursuant to s 79(4)(e). See Zaruba & Zaruba [2017] FamCAFC 91; (2017) FLC 93-776 at 77,312.
(Citation included)
I will proceed on the basis the wife really sought a s 75(2)(o) adjustment, as opposed to a s 79(4) contributions based adjustment.
In Bokin & Wild,[126] the Appeal Division has reminded:
51. If the appellant sought to pursue a claim based on Robb & Robb it was incumbent upon him to adduce evidence which was sufficient for the primary judge to assess and weigh that claim. He did not.

(Emphasis added)
Apart from the bare fact of them spending time with the husband and wife on alternate weekends and holidays, the wife did not put on any evidence which would allow for an assessment and weighting of that claim per Bokin. I do not even know how long those parenting arrangements lasted and what the wife did for them. I was also not enlightened on what I make of the accepted fact that the wife was paid back the money she used to buy the husband’s son a car, and received more of the Suburb H property because, inter alia, she spent money on a trip to Disneyland.
A much earlier Full Court said in R & H[127] that:
There is a danger of double counting in too readily making such an adjustment. Where each party brings in equivalent capital and the efforts of each party during the course of the marriage are seen to be equal, the fact that children from another relationship benefited in some way by support given to them arising out of that relationship, does not necessarily lead to the conclusion that a further adjustment should be made on behalf of the non-parent. One cannot necessarily conclude that, absent the step-children, the parties would be any the richer. Their quality of life may have been enhanced in terms of expenditure on themselves rather than on the children, or there may have been opportunity for capital gain or savings, but these things are merely speculative. Ultimately it is a matter for the discretion of the trial Judge.
There are further difficulties with the so-called Robb v Robb concept. Whilst it is true that a step-parent has no legal obligation to support a step-child unless an order has been made under s 66M of the Family Law Act, a moral obligation may well be created before then. In any event, the relationship between a step-parent and a step-child is not necessarily a one way street. Nor is it one upon which it is necessarily appropriate to put any commercial value.
(Emphasis added)
I also accept what the Full Court said in Zaruba & Zaruba[128] that:
... not everything a party does for the benefit of their spouse’s children should result in some monetary reward in property settlement proceedings.
I was not enlightened as to what, if anything, I make of the fact that the second respondent (albeit as an adult-child of the first respondent) lived rent free in properties for considerable periods.
I accept the wife had some involvement in the husband’s children’s care, but the evidence does not allow for any safe assessment being made of whatever she might have done or weighting to give.
There are no s 75(2) factors which warrant an adjustment in either the husband’s or wife’s favour, and I find accordingly. I accept the parties’ submissions in this regard, save I have considered the wife’s Robb & Robb claim under s 75(2)(o) not s 79(4) as she sought.

Disposition and Orders

Orders were made on 9 April 2025 for an updated valuation of the Suburb C property.
Those Orders were not complied with. The second respondent took great umbrage with the notion of non-compliance, saying he was “unable” to comply. That is splitting hairs.
At earlier court dates, the Court was at pains to explain to the parties that if certain findings were made, and if there was no updated valuation of the property, then an option for the Court may be to order the sale of the property to ascertain an up-to-date valuation. None of the parties could be under any illusion that that was not an option.
In the October 2025 tranche of the trial, the two respondents agreed to the applicant’s request to update the valuation of the Suburb C home.[129] The parties were also advised by the Court that:
HER HONOUR: ... if six weeks comes and goes and I don’t have a joint valuation, then I’m going to work on the basis that I can just start [these reasons] and I will do the best I can with what I’ve got. That suits you, Ms Hamilton?

MR LIVINGSTONE: Thank you, your Honour.

MS HAMILTON: Certainly, your Honour.

HER HONOUR: Good. Thank you. All right. So, [Mr V Zhivko], [judgement] it isn’t going to be tomorrow – of course it won’t be tomorrow, Saturday – I’ve got a bit to do. All right?

[MR V ZHIVKO]: Yes, your Honour.

(Transcript 10 October 2025, p.142 lines 29-43)
On 4 December 2025 at 5.15 pm, Chambers emailed the parties asking about the updated valuation.
On 4 December 2025, at 6.34 pm the second respondent replied:
Dear Associate,

I wish to advise that the Second Respondent has not received any correspondence in relation to following up on this matter from either the Applicant’s or the First Respondent’s legal representatives since that date.

I also wish to advise that it is my understanding that the last advise [sic] given by both parties earlier this year was that neither had the funds then to pay for the additional court ordered valuation of the subject property to be carried out at that time.

Im [sic] currently unaware if this position has changed for the Applicant or the First Respondent, having not heard from either party since the conclusion of the last attendance dates.

Regards,

[Mr V Zhivko]
On 9 December 2025 at 9.33 am, the solicitor for the wife replied to Chambers saying:
Dear Associate,

RE: SYC5015/2021 – [Ludas & Zhivko] [SEC=OFFICIAL]

I refer to the above matter and to your email correspondence below.

I confirm that I act for the First Respondent, [Ms Zhivko].

I am instructed by my client that she is not in a financial position to fund the costs of an updated valuation.

(Emphasis in original)
Given the concession by the two respondents that the property now falls into the pool available for division (even though the first respondent said their ought be no adjustment by either Stanford considerations or the ‘usual’ s 79 process), and given the valuation is about 16 months old, it is difficult to see how the Court could do justice and equity to the parties by using that old valuation.
Indeed, in Goncharov & Goncharova,[130] the Appeal Division of this Court made it clear that the appropriate time to value assets of the parties, or either of them, is as close to the trial as possible.[131] That is consistent with a long line of authorities including the Full Court in AJO & GRO [132] at [16]:
...The starting point is that ordinarily, in proceedings under s 79 of [the Act] the property and financial resources of the parties are valued as at the date of the trial...
There is no evidence before me that the first respondent has any capacity to borrow money to pay out the husband.[133] Both the applicant and first respondent are in parlous financial circumstances. Indeed, the wife’s solicitors indicated the wife could not afford to contribute to the updated valuation. Thus, giving the wife the opportunity to raise monies to pay out the husband is futile. Instead, an order for the sale of the property and a simple method to calculate who gets what, taking account of what each the applicant and first respondent have in their possession, is the only viable way to do justice between the applicant and first respondent.
I appreciate a sale means the second respondent and his family will need to move home, but: (a) he has long been aware that the sale of the property was an option; (b) he stifled the updated valuation orders of 9 April 2025 saying he was “unable to comply”; and, (c) he, like his mother, abandoned their trust case.
The husband sought an order pursuant to s 106B of the Act, that the document called a “Deed of Agreement” dated 1 May 2022 entered into by the first and second respondents be set aside. Having abandoned their trust case, that order is unnecessary. It is also the case, that the purported Deed is not in registerable form and is so vague as to be meaningless.[134] Instead, should the first and/or second respondents wish to rely upon it for some purpose or assert the second respondent has an interest in the property, then the husband has leave to show the relevant entity /institution these Orders and Reasons.
As the husband was the only party with sale orders, I will largely make the orders he proposed. Both respondents had notice of those orders and could have made submissions about them. He does not propose an order requiring the wife to accept an offer which meets the listing price or reserve price. Even though that would seem to go without saying, I will give the parties liberty to apply on short notice in relation to the implementation or interpretation of these Orders. I will also make an Order to make it clear that the husband and wife do not need to engage with each other with respect to implementation of these Orders, but may do so via their legal representatives.
I will give the applicant (if required to participate) and first respondent 28 days from the date of this Order to arrange for the property to be listed. I will then give three months from the listing for sale by private treaty. If the property is not sold within those three months, the property will be auctioned.
That gives the second respondent plenty of time to get his affairs in order and move prior to settlement – in addition to the fact he has been on notice that sale was a possible outcome since at least April last year, 2025.
I will also make Orders directed at the second respondent that he allow inspections and open homes of the property as requested by the agent and that he and his family vacate the property prior to settlement.
The pool is $888,001, but that figure will ebb or flow with the sale price.
On 70 percent to the wife, she will receive in the vicinity of $621,601 (depending on the sale proceeds).
On 30 percent to the husband, he will receive $266,400 (again, depending on the sale proceeds).
I accept that is a differential of 40 percent and $355,201 in monetary terms (but subject to sale proceeds).
Standing back, I consider the proposed outcome is just and equitable per s 79(2) given all the matters discussed in these Reasons.
For all the foregoing Reasons, I am satisfied the Orders set out at the commencement of these Reasons should be made and that they are appropriate.
